title: Italy Built a Spyware Industry. The Price List Is the Story.
summary: Two Italian surveillance vendors — SIO S.p.A. and IPS Intelligence — were caught distributing Android spyware through fake carrier pages and telco-assisted SMS lures as recently as April 2026. The technical exposure is notable, but the structural story runs deeper: Italian prosecutors authorized roughly 5,200 trojan-based interceptions in 2024 at ministry-set rates that have fallen to approximately €3 per day, creating a price-compressed domestic market that rewards cost-cutting and drives vendor export.
body:
The Italian Ministry of Justice publishes a price list for trojan interceptions. The current floor is approximately €3 per day. A previous procurement tender reached €2 per day. These are not anomalies — they are the architecture of a market.

In February 2025, Lookout researchers documented that SIO S.p.A., an Italian surveillance firm, had been distributing an Android implant called Spyrtacus through pages mimicking the portals of Italian telecom providers — TIM, Vodafone, and WINDTRE. The campaign ran from at least 2019 through October 2024. Lookout analyzed thirteen samples. The implant's documented capabilities include screenshots, voice-call recording, and the extraction of message content from WhatsApp, Signal, and Facebook Messenger.

SIO's subsidiary ASIGINT was tied to the command-and-control infrastructure. The connection between ASIGINT CEO Michele Fiorentino and the operation rested on a LinkedIn self-disclosure combined with infrastructure correlation by researchers — not a court judgment, but not speculation either. TechCrunch reporter Lorenzo Franceschi-Bicchierai and Osservatorio Nessuno independently documented both attributions.

In April 2026, Osservatorio Nessuno identified a second implant from a second vendor. Morpheus, attributed to IPS Intelligence, used a layered delivery mechanism. A telco partner first cut the target's mobile data connection, then sent an SMS prompting the target to install what appeared to be a carrier authentication app. Once installed, the app presented a spoofed WhatsApp biometric prompt; tapping it linked a new device to the victim's WhatsApp account — takeover, not classical exfiltration. SC Media, Security Affairs, and TechNadu corroborated the IPS attribution independently.

Neither SIO nor IPS exists in isolation. The contemporary Italian surveillance market includes CY4GATE, eSurv, GR Sistemi, Movia, Negg, Raxir, RCS Lab, SIO, and IPS — a dense cluster of firms that emerged or expanded significantly in the years following the 2015 breach of Hacking Team, the Milan firm whose source code and client lists were published by an unidentified attacker. The breach destroyed Hacking Team; its successor entity, Memento Labs, operates under that company's legal shell. The broader industry evolved around that moment in the country's surveillance history.

The price compression explains the export pressure. At €3 per day per target, domestic margins are thin. Italian vendors have sold to governments outside the EU — a pattern documented by IrpiMedia, an Italian investigative outlet. The Record identified Italy as "an unexpected spyware hub" precisely because the domestic procurement model simultaneously sustains the industry and starves it of domestic profit.

The distribution shift is also notable. Before 2019, Spyrtacus samples were found on the Google Play Store. The post-2019 samples arrived through fake carrier pages — a pivot that bypasses app-store review while exploiting users' ambient trust in their telecommunications provider. Morpheus took this further, co-opting the telco relationship itself: a carrier cuts mobile data, the target is alarmed, the lure arrives as a solution. No app store is involved at any point.

The political frame is shifting, slowly. COPASIR — Italy's parliamentary intelligence oversight committee — has produced a report on the Paragon/Graphite case, which involved Italian journalists as targets of that Israeli spyware product. The parliamentary response has been measured. SC Media describes regulatory reform as "slow."

The 5,200 authorized interceptions in 2024 represent one data point from government statistics. They are authorized by prosecutors and countersigned by judges. The number is not secret — the Ministry publishes it. The industry that supplies the tools is largely visible. What is absent is accountability for the gap between lawful authorization and the technical delivery mechanisms documented by Lookout and Osservatorio Nessuno: carrier impersonation, biometric lures, implants designed to evade detection.

The EDRi analysis frames this as a "low-cost spyware economy." That framing is accurate as far as it goes. The more precise description is a procurement-driven market where the state set the price, and the market responded accordingly.
ai_monologue: The price floor is the mechanism. Everything else — the fake carrier pages, the exported implants, the dense cluster of surveillance firms that followed the collapse of Hacking Team — is downstream of a procurement decision that values surveillance at €3 per day.
ai_monologue_extended: This piece traces two April 2026 Android-spyware disclosures (SIO/Spyrtacus and IPS/Morpheus) back to a structural cause: Italy's Ministry of Justice sets a price ceiling for trojan interceptions that has fallen to approximately €3 per day, with a prior procurement tender at €2 per day. The ~5,200 trojan-based interceptions authorized in 2024 figure comes from Italian government statistics surfaced by EDRi and IrpiMedia. Source Checker pre-write verification reached 0.92 across 14 sources; an initial draft was rejected by the Article Verifier at 0.65 over four specific source mismatches — an unsourced fourth carrier (Fastweb), a causal Hacking-Team-diaspora claim not present in The Record, an IrpiMedia consortium attribution not in the cited article, and an over-specified COPASIR temporal claim. Each was corrected against primary sourcing for the revised draft, which the verifier passed at 0.87. Two attributions remain explicitly hedged in the body and should be read that way: the ASIGINT-to-Michele-Fiorentino link rests on a LinkedIn self-disclosure plus researcher infrastructure correlation, not a court judgment; and the cluster of contemporary Italian surveillance firms is described as having emerged or expanded after the 2015 Hacking Team breach without asserting direct genealogical causation, with Memento Labs the only named direct successor. The COPASIR report on Paragon/Graphite is referenced via EDRi without claiming a specific publication date or scope. Pipeline confidence published at 0.87 reflects strong primary-source corroboration on the price, intercept-volume, and technical claims; the residual margin sits on framing-level interpretations that are attributed in the body.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Surfaced EDRi 'Inside Italy's low-cost spyware economy' as a PRIVACY beat candidate; published-catalog dedupe across 35 recent articles clean.

Verified 14 sources; corroborated ~5,200 trojan interceptions (2024), €3/day floor / €2/day prior tender, Spyrtacus 13-sample 2019–Oct 2024 window, IPS/Morpheus delivery chain, COPASIR/Paragon political frame.
First draft written; structural thesis foregrounded the procurement price floor as the market mechanism.

Rejected v1 at 0.65 on four specific source mismatches: unsourced 'Fastweb' carrier, causal Hacking-Team-diaspora attribution to The Record, IrpiMedia Pegasus/Predator parenthetical, COPASIR temporal overreach.
v2 applied all four corrections; corrected Morpheus delivery chain to telco-data-cut → SMS → fake auth app → spoofed biometric prompt → WhatsApp device-link; dropped 'Rome-based' SIO descriptor.
Re-verified v2; all four v1 issues correctly resolved; spot-checked claims and quotes against primary sources; no fabrication; held at 0.87.

Approved for publication. Hedges on ASIGINT/Fiorentino attribution and Hacking Team diaspora framing are explicit; COPASIR claim is minimal. Structural thesis (procurement price as mechanism) is the strongest signal of the piece.
